plain: A quick assessment of whether a construction job is safe to bid on, based on project size, scope complexity, client type, and timeline. Flags red flags like tight deadlines or unusual scope to help you decide whether to pursue the bid.
A one-page visual map of your entire project — where you are now, what's done, what's next, and when key milestones hit. Open it any time to see the big picture without scrolling through details.
A way to rewind your project to an earlier state — go back and change an estimate, re-run a decision, or undo a change order. Lets you explore what-if scenarios without losing your current progress.

One of seven phases a construction project moves through: Size Up (planning), Lock (paperwork), Plan (crew and permits), Build (execution), Adapt (changes), Collect (payment), Reflect (closeout). Each stage has 2–4 workflows.

The continuous thread connecting all your project data across every workflow — your scope, costs, crew, permits, changes, and notes stay in sync. Add a detail in one workflow and it auto-fills the next one.

Authority Having Jurisdiction — the local government agency (usually the city or county building department) that reviews, approves, and inspects your work. Your permits, plan checks, and inspections all go through them.

Request for Information — a formal written question you send to the architect, engineer, or general contractor when something in the plans is unclear or missing. The AHJ uses RFIs to ask you for clarification during plan check.

Any document, product sample, or certification you give to the AHJ or general contractor for review before installing it — like spec sheets for materials, shop drawings from subs, test reports, or proof of licenses. Submittals lock in what you're actually installing.

A checklist of small jobs that remain incomplete at the end of a project — paint touch-ups, trim, door adjustments, final cleanup. Created during the final walk-through and typically required before the owner accepts the project and releases final payment.

A signed document from a sub, supplier, or laborer saying they've been paid in full and won't file a lien against the property. You need these from everyone before you can get final payment from the owner. Missing one can block your draw for weeks.

A bill you submit to the owner or lender for work completed to date. Based on percentage complete or milestones. The lender or owner reviews, approves, and sends payment. Most projects have 3–6 draws; retainage is held back from each one.

Money held back from each draw (usually 5–10%) until the project is fully complete and all lien waivers are collected. Protects the owner in case you skip town or leave work undone. Released after substantial completion and final inspection.

The AHJ's detailed review of your drawings and documents before you can start work. They look for code violations, missing details, and permit violations. You'll get RFIs for anything unclear; you respond and resubmit until they approve.

California's energy code — rules for insulation, HVAC efficiency, lighting, solar, and water heating. Every project in CA must meet Title 24 compliance. The requirements get stricter every few years. Affects material costs and design decisions.

National Electrical Code — the standard for safe electrical design and installation across the US. Covers wire sizing, breaker protection, grounding, conduit routing, and outlet placement. Your electrician must follow it; the AHJ enforces it during inspection.

International Building Code — the model code for structural safety, fire safety, and life safety adopted by most US states and cities (sometimes with local amendments). Covers everything from foundation loads to egress requirements.

International Plumbing Code — the standard for water supply, drainage, and vent lines. Covers pipe sizing, trap requirements, cleanout placement, and cross-connection protection. Your plumber follows IPC; inspectors verify compliance.

International Residential Code — a comprehensive code for single-family and small multifamily homes, covering structure, energy, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical systems. Simpler than IBC but stricter on some details for residential buildings.

National Fire Protection Association — develops fire and safety codes. NFPA 13 covers sprinkler systems, NFPA 70 is the Electrical Code, NFPA 101 is the Life Safety Code. Used nationwide, often referenced in local codes.

Architecture, Engineering, and Construction — the umbrella term for the whole industry that designs and builds buildings. When you hear 'AEC firms' or 'AEC software,' that means companies/tools serving any of those three trades together.

General Contractor — the licensed company that runs your whole construction project. They hire and manage the subs, pull permits, schedule the work, and are responsible for delivering the finished building. You sign one contract with the GC; they handle the rest.
Subcontractor — a specialist trade (electrician, plumber, framer, drywall, roofer, etc.) hired by the GC to handle one slice of the work. You usually don't talk to subs directly; the GC manages them.

A signed document from a sub, supplier, or laborer saying they've been paid in full and won't file a lien against your property. Your GC needs these from everyone before final payment goes out. Missing one can block your closing or refinance for weeks.

Money the owner holds back from each payment to the GC (usually 5-10%) until the project is fully complete and signed off. It's a safety net — if something's wrong at the end, there's money left to fix it. Released after the final walkthrough.

Construction Specifications Institute — the industry's numbering system for organizing construction costs. Division 03 is Concrete, 06 is Wood, 26 is Electrical, etc. When you see your budget split by '03 Concrete' or '15 Mechanical,' that's CSI.

A bill from your GC for work completed so far. On most jobs, you don't pay everything upfront — the GC bills monthly or by milestone, you (or your lender) review what's done, and pay that portion. Most projects have 3-8 draws total.

A written amendment to your contract when scope changes mid-project — you decide to add a window, the slab hits rock and needs more excavation, you upgrade to quartz countertops. Each change order spells out: what changed, how much more it costs, and how many extra days. Sign before the work happens.

The detailed list of exactly what's included in your project — every wall, every fixture, every finish. 'In scope' means the GC has to do it for the contract price; 'out of scope' means it's extra. Most fights between owners and GCs come from fuzzy scope.

A placeholder dollar amount in your contract for something you haven't picked yet — like '$8,000 tile allowance' or '$15,000 cabinet allowance.' If you pick something cheaper, you save the difference; pricier, you pay the upcharge as a change order. Watch the allowances — they hide budget overruns.

Time and Materials — a billing structure where you pay the GC for actual hours worked plus the cost of materials, instead of a fixed price. Used for unpredictable work like demo or repairs where nobody knows what's behind the wall yet. Higher risk for the owner — no cap on cost.

A single fixed price for the whole project, no matter how long it takes or what materials cost. The GC takes the risk — if they underestimated, they eat the loss; if they're efficient, they keep the savings. Most homeowner contracts are lump sum because it's predictable.

Project costs that aren't construction itself — architect/engineer fees, permits, plan check fees, surveys, financing fees, insurance, legal. Usually 10-25% of total project cost. Easy to forget; budget them upfront.

The actual construction costs — labor, materials, equipment. The stuff that becomes the building. Roughly 75-90% of total project cost. When the GC gives you a number, that's hard costs unless they say otherwise.

Certificate of Insurance — a one-page document proving your GC (or sub) actually has the insurance they say they have: general liability, workers' comp, auto. Demand a fresh one before work starts, and ask to be listed as an additional insured. If they balk, that's a red flag.

The industry-standard payment application forms from the American Institute of Architects. G702 is the summary page (this draw, total to date, retainage); G703 is the detailed breakdown by cost code. Most lenders require these forms for every draw on a financed project.

California's Home Improvement Contract law. Spells out exactly what has to be in a residential remodel contract over $500: down payment limits (10% or $1,000, whichever is less), required notices, 3-day right to cancel, finance disclosures. If your CA contractor skips B&P 7159 requirements, the contract may be unenforceable against you.

The California Civil Code section that defines the legally valid form of a conditional lien waiver upon progress payment. When your GC's subs hand you waivers, they need to match this exact statutory language or they don't actually waive lien rights.
pro: California Civil Code §8132 conditional progress waiver

plain: California's energy code — the rules for insulation, HVAC efficiency, lighting, solar panels, and water heating that every CA project has to meet. Stricter every few years. You'll see line items for 'T24 calcs' (a consultant runs the energy model) and 'T24 inspection' (the AHJ verifies installed equipment matches the model).

California Residential Code — California's version of the IRC, with CA-specific amendments (seismic bracing, T24 energy, water conservation). The CRC governs single-family homes, duplexes, and townhomes. Your GC and the building inspector both work from the same CRC.

The code classification for residential occupancies — single-family homes, duplexes, small apartments. When you see 'R-3' on plans or permits, it means the building falls under residential code (CRC/IRC), not commercial. Different rules for fire separation, egress, and sprinklers than R-1 (hotels) or R-2 (larger apartments).

Accessory Dwelling Unit — a second, smaller home on a single-family lot. Could be a detached cottage in the back yard, a unit above the garage, or a basement conversion. California law makes ADUs much easier to permit than other additions: streamlined plan check, no extra parking requirements, can be rented separately.

A Junior ADU is a smaller carve-out from your existing home — up to 500 sqft, must include an efficiency kitchen, can share a bathroom with the main house. Easier and cheaper than a full ADU because you're not adding to the building footprint. One JADU is allowed per lot in CA, on top of the regular ADU rules.

California Senate Bill 9 (effective 2022) — lets owners of single-family lots split the lot in two and/or build a duplex, by-right, in most CA cities. Bypasses much of the usual zoning review. If your lot qualifies and you follow the rules (size, owner-occupancy, etc.), the city can't say no.
pro: California Senate Bill 9 (2021) — Urban Lot Split

plain: California Assembly Bill 68 (2019) — the law that opened up ADUs statewide. Limited cities' ability to block them, killed the parking requirement near transit, capped permit timelines, and required ministerial (no-discretion) approval. The reason ADUs exploded in CA.
